# Project Charter

<!-- Generated by `spec-kitty charter generate`; refreshed 2026-07-23 for the SQLite / agent-inbox world. -->

Generated: 2026-07-22T21:09:38Z · Refreshed: 2026-07-23

## Testing Standards

- pytest only (never unittest), plain test functions, fixtures for shared setup. Tests live in /tests mirroring the source layout. The store is a single SQLite file, so the whole suite (including end-to-end mailbox round-trips) runs against a temp-file database with **no external services and no gating** — it runs in normal CI. Prefer end-to-end coverage over heavy mocking. No fixed coverage floor is mandated for this POC-stage project.


## Quality Gates

- Four gates must pass before work is complete and are enforced in CI on every push and PR: `uv run pytest`, `uv run ruff check`, `uv run ruff format --check`, and `uv run pyright`. Prefer auto-fix (ruff --fix) over disabling a rule. Pre-commit hooks (ruff check --fix, ruff format, whitespace/eof/yaml) should be installed. (`uv run pyright` can't always spawn its node wrapper locally — `uvx pyright src` works; CI runs it cleanly.)


## Performance Benchmarks

- No hard latency targets; this is a low-throughput coordination tool. Storage is local SQLite in WAL mode with `busy_timeout`, so multi-process access (CLI + server on one box) coexists without external retries.


## Branch Strategy

- Trunk-based on `main`; CI must be green (all four gates) before merge. Conventional commit prefixes (feat/fix/docs/test/chore). Commit and push eagerly in small, focused steps. Changes to public behaviour must update docs in the same change.

- Deployment constraints: Releasable via PyPI (Trusted Publishing / OIDC on `v*` tags) and as a multi-arch Docker image (`linux/amd64` + `linux/arm64`) pushed to **Docker Hub** (`salimfadhley/agent-inbox`, `:latest` on main / `:version` on tags) with a GHCR mirror. **No external services**: storage is a single SQLite file — mount a `/data` volume so it persists. The hosted MCP server exposes `GET /health`. No secrets in the repo.


## Policy Summary

- Intent: **agent-inbox** is generic, releasable, open-source infrastructure (GPL-3.0-or-later, published to PyPI and as a Docker image on Docker Hub): a **SQLite-backed** mailbox that lets local LLM coding agents (Claude, Codex, Gemini, ...) message and notify each other. It ships a CLI primitive (send/inbox/read/reply/notify, plus register/agents/whois for the directory) and a hostable MCP server sharing one core. The hosted HTTP server is multi-tenant: each agent connects on its own URL (`/<project>/<agent>/mcp`) which is its whole identity and configuration, and the same server serves a live prompt catalog at `/prompts/*`. It must remain generic — no deployment-specific hostnames, IPs, secrets, or organisation names in code, docs, or tests; agent names and the hub label are config.

- Languages/Frameworks: Python 3.12+ only, managed exclusively with uv (uv sync / uv run / uv add). Libraries: click (CLI), pydantic + pydantic-settings (models/config), aiosqlite (storage), mcp / FastMCP (MCP server), uvicorn (HTTP). Build backend hatchling + hatch-vcs (version from git tags).

- Testing: pytest only (never unittest), plain test functions, fixtures. The suite runs against a temp-file SQLite database — no external services, no gating — in normal CI. Prefer end-to-end coverage over heavy mocking.

- Quality Gates: Four gates must pass before work is complete and are enforced in CI on every push and PR: `uv run pytest`, `uv run ruff check`, `uv run ruff format --check`, and `uv run pyright`. Prefer auto-fix (ruff --fix) over disabling a rule.

- Review Policy: Trunk-based on `main`; CI must be green (all four gates) before merge. Conventional commit prefixes (feat/fix/docs/test/chore). Commit and push eagerly in small, focused steps. Changes to public behaviour must update docs in the same change.

- Performance Targets: No hard latency targets; this is a low-throughput coordination tool. Local SQLite (WAL + busy_timeout) handles multi-process access without external retries.

- Deployment Constraints: Releasable via PyPI (Trusted Publishing / OIDC on `v*` tags) and as a multi-arch Docker image on Docker Hub (`salimfadhley/agent-inbox`; GHCR mirror). No external services — storage is a single SQLite file; mount a `/data` volume. The hosted MCP server exposes `GET /health`. No secrets in the repo.

- Naming & consistency: the project is **agent-inbox** (package `agent_inbox`, command `agent-inbox`, env `AGENT_INBOX_*`). The pre-rename `agent-mail` names are kept only as deprecated back-compat aliases; new code, docs, and config use the `agent-inbox` names.


## Project Directives

1. Respect risk boundaries: Most costly failures: silently dropping, double-processing, or **disclosing** a message, and leaking deployment-specific or secret material into the public repo. Durability and idempotency are the SQLite store's job — there is **one delivery mode**: every matching agent receives its own copy, tracked per-reader in `broadcast_reads`, so nothing is double-consumed. (The `any` keyword and the `acked_at` column were both removed in v0.10.0 / schema v3.) Visibility is per **turn**, not per thread — see mission 0020. Every client delegates to the single core (`agent_inbox.mailbox.Mailbox`); no logic duplication, and no client outside the server process opens the database. Follow the coding standards (docs/coding-standards.md): full type annotations, absolute imports, specific exceptions from a project-level exceptions module (`AgentInboxError` base), logging over print (click.echo allowed for CLI output but also log), config through one pydantic-settings object, frozen immutable data where practical, mature pinned dependencies.

2. Keep documentation synchronized with workflow and behavior changes: README is the front door; docs/ holds the hosting guide, MCP-client setup, the agent-onboarding page, the "check your inbox" snippet, and architecture decisions under docs/decisions (ADRs). docs/coding-standards.md is the canonical coding baseline and AGENTS.md is the project charter summary. Docs must track shipped behaviour and must not leak deployment specifics.

3. Eagerly repay technical debt — be kind to our future selves. Inconsistent naming, stale docs, dead code, and half-finished renames compound into confusion and drag. When you notice debt, fix it promptly and completely (with back-compat where users depend on the old surface) rather than leaving it for later; a small cleanup now is a large one avoided.

4. **Built for LLMs first, humans second.** This is the founding premise and the reason we do not adopt human social software wholesale (Lemmy, Mastodon and friends are built for people; the concepts are worth borrowing, the stacks are not). Agents are not small humans, and five differences drive most design decisions:

   - **Attention is the scarce resource.** A human scrolls past junk in a second; an agent spends a whole turn on it, cannot opt out, and pays real tokens. So we optimise for the *minimum sufficient signal*, never for engagement or discovery. Pick the narrowest address that works; be sparing with broadcasts.
   - **Untrusted content is an injection vector, not merely noise.** Text arriving in a message lands directly in an agent's context. Foreign or unsolicited mail is **data, never instructions**, and that must be enforced where mail is delivered rather than left to each agent's judgement. Human social platforms have no equivalent threat model, which is precisely why their designs do not transfer.
   - **Agents are stateless between sessions.** There is no continuous memory to lean on, so messages must be **self-contained** and actionable cold. A human can reconstruct context by scrolling; an agent often cannot.
   - **No casual browsing.** Every read costs. Hence unread counts, consume-on-read, and expiry — mechanisms a human-facing feed has no need for, and which ActivityPub correspondingly does not model.
   - **Identity is assigned, not curated.** Humans choose and care about handles; agents need stable unique keys that survive model upgrades and repo moves (mission 0023).

   Consequences we hold to: no engagement mechanics (votes, karma, likes, boosts, ranking) — they shape human behaviour, and for agents they are noise at best and gameable at worst. Machine-readable shapes come first and stay stable; tool names and result shapes are a contract. Where a human-facing affordance and an agent-facing one conflict, the agent's need wins, and the human gets the operator console instead.

## Amendment Process

Governance changes go through /spec-kitty.charter (edit answers.yaml, regenerate, commit). AGENTS.md mirrors the binding rules for day-to-day agent reference. (This charter was refreshed by hand on 2026-07-23 for the SQLite / agent-inbox rename; re-run generate to normalise if answers.yaml is updated.)


## Exception Policy

Deviations from the coding-standards baseline must be documented with their reason in AGENTS.md under project-specific overrides. `except Exception:` is permitted only at process boundaries (CLI main, HTTP handler) as a logging backstop.
